elements.sent-2411-001: For a generalization of the Gellerstedt operator with mixed-type Dirichlet boundary conditions to a suitable Tricomi domain, we prove the existence and uniqueness of weak solutions of the linear problem and for a generalization of this problem.
elements.sent-2411-002: The classical method introduced by Didenko, which study the energy integral argument, will be used to prove estimates for a specific Tricomi domain.
elements.sent-2411-003: Consider the following problem involving a Gellerstedt type operator with mixed-type Dirichlet boundary conditions \begin{equation}\label{LPL} \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(u)-\lambda u:=-y^{m_1}u_{xx}-x^{m_2}u_{yy}-\lambda u=f(x,y) in\quad \Omega, \quad \quad \quad \quad u=\gamma on\quad AC\cup\sigma\subseteq\partial\Omega, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $m_1,m_2\in \mathbb{N ,$ $f\in L^2(\Omega)$ and $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R ^2$ is a Tricomi domain for the operator $\mathcal{O ,$ that is, $\Omega$ is open, bounded, simply connected set with piecewise $C^1$ boundary $\partial\Omega=\sigma\cup AC\cup BC$ formed by the curve $\sigma\subset\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R ^2; \ y>0\}$ is a piecewise $C^2$ simple arc join the points $A=(-x_1,0)$ and $B=(x_0,0)$ with $0<x_0<x_1$ in $\mathbb{R}$ and the characteristics curves $AC$ and $BC$ in $\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R ^2; \ -x_1\leq x\leq x_0 \ and \ y\leq0\$ of $\mathcal{O}$ passing through points $A$ and $B$ respectively and meet at point $C$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{LPL}  O(u)-\lambda u:=-y^{m_1}u_{xx}-x^{m_2}u_{yy}-\lambda u=f(x,y) in\quad \Omega, \quad \quad \quad \quad u=\gamma on\quad AC\cup\sigma\subseteq\partial\Omega, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-005: As developed in \cite{olirodcar , the characteristic curves $AC$ and $BC$ in $\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R ^2; \ y\leq0\}$ of the operator $\mathcal{O}$, for $m_1\in\mathbb{N}$ odd and $\frac{m_2 {2}\in\mathbb{N}$ even, are given by \begin{equation}\label{AC1} AC=\bigg\{(x,y)\inR^2; \ y_c\leq y\leq0, \nonumber \frac{2}{m_1+2}(-y)^\frac{m_1+2}{2}=\frac{2}{m_2+2}\left[x^\frac{m_2+2}{2}+x_1^\frac{m_2+2}{2}\right] \bigg\},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{BC1} BC=\bigg\{ (x,y)\inR^2; \ y_c\leq y\leq0, \nonumber \frac{2}{m_1+2}(-y)^\frac{m_1+2}{2}=-\frac{2}{m_2+2}\left[x^\frac{m_2+2}{2}-x_0^\frac{m_2+2}{2}\right] \bigg\},\end{equation} and the point $C=(x_c, y_c)$ where \begin{equation}x_c=-\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(x_1^\frac{m_2+2}{2}-x_0^\frac{m_2+2}{2}\right)\right]^\frac{2}{m_2+2}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}y_c=-\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{m_1+2}{m_2+2}\right)(x_1^\frac{m_2+2}{2}-x_0^\frac{m_2+2}{2})\right]^\frac{2}{m_1+2}\cdot\end{equation} Parameterizing \eqref{AC1 and \eqref{BC1 with respect to the variable $y$, the exterior normal vector will be given by \begin{equation}\eta_{AC}=\left(-1,-\left[\frac{m_2+2}{m_1+2}(-y)^\frac{m_1+2}{2}-x_1^\frac{m_2+2}{2}\right]^\frac{-m_2}{m_2+2}(-y)^\frac{m_1}{2}\right),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\eta_{BC}=\left(1,-\left[-\frac{m_2+2}{m_1+2}(-y)^\frac{m_1+2}{2}+x_0^\frac{m_2+2}{2}\right]^\frac{-m_2}{m_2+2}(-y)^\frac{m_1}{2}\right),\end{equation} respectively.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The importance in the study problem \eqref{LPL is due to the operator $\mathcal{O =-y^{m_1}\partial_{xx -x^{m_2}\partial_{yy}$ is a generalization of the operators Laplacian operator $\Delta=\partial_{xx +\partial_{yy}$, Tricomi operator $T=y\partial_{xx +\partial_{yy}$ see \cite{lupopayne, Moiseev, Sabitova , and Gellerstedt operator $L=y^{m_1}\partial_{xx +\partial_{yy}$, see\cite{lupopayne, Moiseev,Sabitova , for that reason the existence results of weak solutions to the problem \eqref{LPL , were motivated by the classical result involving the Laplacian operator in \cite{giltru and the linear problem involving the Tricomi operator in \cite{didenko .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Nonexistence result of nontrivial regular solutions for the semilinear problem involving the operator $\mathcal{O}$ in \cite{olirodcar plays an important role in our study, since some arguments made on the Tricomi domains as well as in the weighted Sobolev spaces associated with the operator were borrowed from there.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Briefly we are going make a review of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-009: In \cite{Otway Otway treated the Keldysh type operator getting existence of distribution solution also some maximum principle for non nuniformly elliptic operator.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Sabitova in \cite{Sabitova studied a eigenvalue problem involving Chaplygin type operator \begin{equation}(sign \, y) |y|^m u_{xx} +u_{yy}-\lambda |y|^m u=0\end{equation} where uniqueness and non existence results for $\lambda$ in some range.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In \cite{Favaron,Moiseev also eigenfunctions problem is treated.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In \cite{Paronetto existence for elliptic-hyperbolic Tricomi problem is established, while in \cite{Chen is obtained estimates for Dirichlet eigenvalue for degenerate $\Delta_{\mu}$ Laplace operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: All previously mentioned operators, except for the Laplacian, share a common feature in terms of a weight.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, our operator involves two weights.
elements.sent-2411-015: This additional weight brings others difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-016: In fact, the gradient and the norm are always related by the divergence and this plays a crucial role in the study of PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-018: Along with this, we add the non-zero lambda in \eqref{LPL with a view to studying the spectral theory of this operator in the future.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Following Didenko's ideas in \cite{didenko , we will study the particular linear problem where $\lambda=\gamma=0$ in \eqref{LPL on the same domain $\Omega$. \begin{equation}\label{LP} \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(u):=-y^{m_1}u_{xx}-x^{m_2}u_{yy}=f(x,y) in\quad \Omega, \quad\quad u=0 on\quad AC\cup\sigma\subseteq\partial\Omega. \end{cases}\end{equation} and consider also on the same domain $\Omega$ the adjoint problem to the semilinear problem \eqref{LP \begin{equation}\label{ALP} \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(v):=-y^{m_1}v_{xx}-x^{m_2}v_{yy}=f(x,y) in\quad \Omega, \quad\quad v=0 on\quad BC\cup\sigma\subseteq\partial\Omega. \end{cases}\end{equation} Throughout the development of this paper, as is inevitable, we found several difficulties, mainly with respect to domain $\Omega$ which we impose some modifications to get suitable estimates.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: No less important and not mentioned in previous works to ours, as for the Gellerstedt operator as well for the Tricomi operators, is the interaction between the admissibility of the domain for the operator $\mathcal{O}$ and consequently for the operator $\mathcal{O -\lambda$ with $\lambda\leq \frac{1 {K_3 ,$ where $K_3$ is a suitable constant.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Let $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R ^2$ be a admissible Tricomi domain for the operator $\mathcal{O -\lambda$ as defined in the problem \eqref{LPL , $f(x,y)\in L^2(\Omega)$, $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ and $\lambda\leq0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The problem \begin{equation}\label{CREYS_1} \begin{cases} \mathcal{O}(u)-\lambda u=f(x,y) in\quad \Omega, \quad\quad\quad\quad u=\gamma on\quad AC\cup\sigma\subseteq\partial\Omega, \end{cases}\end{equation} admits an unique weak solution and the solution operator \begin{equation}S^{\lambda,\gamma}_{AC\cup\sigma}: L^2(\Omega)\rightarrow W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}\end{equation} which assigns to $f\in L^2(\Omega)$ the unique weak solution $u\in W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}$ of the problem \eqref{CREYS_1 is linear and continuous.}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{CREYS_1}  O(u)-\lambda u=f(x,y) in\quad \Omega, \quad\quad\quad\quad u=\gamma on\quad AC\cup\sigma\subseteq\partial\Omega, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-024: This paper will be divided into two parts.
elements.sent-2411-025: In section \ref{sec2 , we define an admissible Tricomi domain, then we will prove the existence of an admissible domain for the operator $\mathcal{O}$ and consequently for the operator $\mathcal{O-\lambda}$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We present the spaces associated to the mentioned problems and their respective dual space in order to analyze how they are related and obtain a better understanding of our operator.
elements.sent-2411-027: In section \ref{sec3 , we will present the main results in this paper, we start prove uniqueness and existence of the weak solution for the problem \eqref{LP and after with the help of this we proof the uniqueness and existence of the weak solutions for the problem \eqref{LPL .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this section we will define spaces weighted Sobolev naturally associated with the problems \eqref{LP and \eqref{ALP , their dual spaces and their norms, as well as, we will define a specific Tricomi domains for our work and we will prove the existence of this domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Consider, \begin{equation}C_{AC\cup\sigma}^{\infty}(\overline{\Omega})=\{u\in C^\infty(\overline{\Omega}); \ \ u|_{AC\cup\sigma}=0\},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}C_{BC\cup\sigma}^{\infty}(\overline{\Omega})=\{v\in C^\infty(\overline{\Omega}); \ \ v|_{BC\cup\sigma}=0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-030: Define $W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}$ and $W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}$ the closures of $C_{AC\cup\sigma ^{\infty (\overline{\Omega )$ and $C_{BC\cup\sigma ^{\infty (\overline{\Omega )$ with respect to the norm of $W^{1,2 (\Omega),$ respectively.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: By the theory of space with negative norm \cite{lax consider the dual space $W^{-1 _{AC\cup\sigma}$ and $W^{-1 _{BC\cup\sigma}$ of $W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}$ and $W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}$ respectively with the norm \begin{equation}||\varphi||_{W^{-1}_{AC\cup\sigma}}= \sup_{0\neq u\in W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}}\frac{|(\varphi,u)_{L^2}|}{||u||_{W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}}},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}||\phi||_{W^{-1}_{BC\cup\sigma}}= \sup_{0\neq v\in W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}}\frac{|(\phi,v)_{L^2}|}{||v||_{W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}}}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We have the following inclusions \begin{equation}W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}\subset L^2(\Omega)\subset W^{-1}_{AC\cup\sigma} \ \ \ and \ \ \ W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}\subset L^2(\Omega)\subset W^{-1}_{BC\cup\sigma}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}\subset L^2(\Omega)\subset W^{-1}_{AC\cup\sigma} \ \ \ and \ \ \ W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}\subset L^2(\Omega)\subset W^{-1}_{BC\cup\sigma}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-033: In \cite{olirodcar , we defined $\mathcal{X u=(-y^{m_1 u_x,-x^{m_2 u_y)$ and we observed that $div(\mathcal{X u)=\mathcal{O u$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: By the Divergence Theorem \cite{giltru , we have \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} div(v(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y))=\int_{\partial\Omega} v(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y) \cdot \eta \; ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} div(u(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y))=\int_{\partial\Omega} u(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y) \cdot \eta \; .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-035: By the proprieties of the divergent, we obtain \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} \nabla v(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y)+ \int_{\Omega} v \; div(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y) =\int_{\partial\Omega} v(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y) \cdot \eta \; ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} \nabla u(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y)+ \int_{\Omega} u \; div(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y) =\int_{\partial\Omega} u(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y) \cdot \eta \; ,\end{equation} respectively.
elements.sent-2411-036: Replacing, $\nabla v=(v_x,v_y)$, $\nabla u=(u_x,u_y)$ and $div(\mathcal{X u)=\mathcal{O u,$ we get \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} (v_x,v_y)(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y)+\int_{\Omega} v \; Ou=\int_{\partial\Omega} v(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y) \cdot \eta \; ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} (u_x,u_y)(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y)+\int_{\Omega} u \; Ov=\int_{\partial\Omega} u(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y) \cdot \eta \; .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Therefore, for $u\in W^1_{AC\cup\sigma}$ and $v\in W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}$, we get \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} (v_x,v_y)(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y)+\int_{\Omega} v \; Ou=\int_{AC} v(-y^{m_1}u_x,-x^{m_2}u_y) \cdot \eta \; ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega} (u_x,u_y)(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y)+\int_{\Omega} u \; Ov=\int_{BC} u(-y^{m_1}v_x,-x^{m_2}v_y) \cdot \eta \; .\end{equation}.

In conclusion, \begin{equation}(Ou,v)_{L^2}=\int_{\Omega} v \; Ou=\int_{\Omega} (y^{m_1}u_xv_x+x^{m_2}u_yv_y)=\int_{\Omega} u \; Ov=(u,Ov)_{L^2}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-042: The following definitions, propositions and remarks are adaptations of the definitions found in \cite{lupopayne for our purpose.}
elements.sent-2411-043: A Tricomi domain $\Omega$ is said \textcolor{blue {admissible for the operator $\mathcal{O -\lambda$, if there are positive constants $C_3$ and $C_4$ such that \begin{equation}\label{est3} ||u||_{L^2(\Omega)}\leq C_3||(O_{AC} -\lambda)u||_{W^{-1}_{BC\cup\sigma}}, \ \ u\in W^1_{AC\cup\sigma},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{est4} ||v||_{L^2(\Omega)}\leq C_4||(O_{BC} -\lambda)v||_{W^{-1}_{AC\cup\sigma}}, \ \ v\in W^1_{BC\cup\sigma}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Let $\Omega$ a Tricomi domain with $A=(-x_1,0)$ and $B=(x_0,0)$ with $0<x_0<x_1$ suppose that $\Omega$ and its boundary $\partial\Omega=\sigma\cup AC\cup BC$ satisfies:.
elements.sent-2411-045: The curve $\sigma$ is given by a $C^2$ graph $y=g(x)$ for $-x_1\leq x \leq x_0$ with $g(-x_1)=0=g(x_0)$,.

elements.sent-2411-047: Then $\Omega$ is admissible for the operator $\mathcal{O -\lambda$ for all $\lambda \leq \frac{1 {K_3 ,$ for a suitable $K_3>0.$.}}}
